10: Elisha’s instruction to Naaman — hālôḵ wᵉrāḥaṣtā šeḇaʿ pᵉʿāmîm bayyardēn, ‘go and wash yourself seven times in the Jordan’ — is medically, ritually, and geographically specific. The ṣāraʿat (conventionally translated ‘leprosy’ but covering a range of skin conditions in the Levitical purity system, Lev 13-14) was the purity condition that required a seven-step ritual cleansing in the Levitical law (Leviticus 14:1-9: the cleansed person washes clothes seven days). The number seven in the Elisha instruction echoes Levitical ritual completeness. The Jordan’s choice is geographically pointed: Naaman wants the rivers of Damascus, which are ‘better’ by any measure of water quality (v12: Abana and Pharpar). The Jordan is the covenant-boundary water of Israelite religious geography — the water Joshua crossed (Josh 3), the water John will baptize in, the water at which Elijah and Elisha parted the waters (2 Kings 2:8, 14). The healing occurs in the covenant-water of YHWH’s people, not in the superior rivers of Damascus — signaling that cleanness is defined by YHWH’s covenant, not by natural quality. The seven-fold immersion in covenant-water is the ritual precursor for the NT’s baptismal theology: entry into cleanness through the water of covenant-belonging (Romans 6:4).
17: Naaman’s request for ‘two mule-loads of earth (ʾădāmāh)’ to take back to Aram is a window into standard ANE territorial-deity theology. Throughout the ancient Near East, deities were understood as geographically bound: the gods of a nation were associated with its land, temples, and territory; a worshipper of a particular god who relocated to foreign soil required either the worship of the new territory’s gods or some way of bringing the deity’s territory with them. The Assyrian inscriptions and Egyptian wisdom texts document the assumption that divine power was territorially limited. Naaman’s request is coherent within this framework: to worship YHWH in Damascus requires Israelite soil as the physical substrate of YHWH’s presence. Elisha’s response — lēḵ lᵉšālôm, ‘go in peace’ — neither endorses the theology nor corrects it explicitly; the narrator leaves the theological irony implicit. The prior verse (v15: ‘now I know that there is no God in all the earth but in Israel’) is a more advanced theology than the two-mule-loads request that follows, suggesting Naaman’s understanding is transitional. Isaiah 40:12-31 provides the theological correction: YHWH is not territorially limited but measures the waters in the hollow of his hand; he is the creator of the ends of the earth. John 4:21-24 (Jesus to the Samaritan woman: ‘the hour is coming when neither on this mountain nor in Jerusalem will you worship the Father’) is the final dissolution of the territorial-worship assumption that the two-mule-loads request embodies.
1: Naaman (naʿămān, ‘pleasantness’) is the commander of the Aramean army — śar-ṣᵉbāʾ melek ʾărām — at a time when Aram-Damascus was the dominant power in the southern Levant. The text’s note that ‘through him the LORD had given victory to Aram’ (bô nātan-yhwh tᵉšûʿâ lᵃʾărām) is theologically audacious: YHWH is credited with military victories for a foreign nation, suggesting that YHWH’s sovereignty extends to international politics, not merely Israelite history. His tsara’at — traditionally translated leprosy but more broadly a skin condition causing ritual impurity — would have been a severe disability in a military and court context, affecting his social role without necessarily incapacitating him physically (he is still active as commander).
2: Aramean raiding parties (gᵉdûdîm) into Israel took captives as labor and tribute. The ‘little girl’ (naʿărāh qᵉṭannāh) who serves Naaman’s wife has been separated from her family and homeland by slave raid — a routine feature of Iron Age warfare in the Levant, documented in Assyrian records as well as biblical narrative. Her ethnic identity as Israelite is significant: the knowledge of YHWH’s prophet in Samaria exists at the margins of Aramean power, carried by a nameless enslaved child. The narrative’s structure places the reversal of Israel’s powerlessness in the mouth of its most vulnerable member.
3: The girl’s declaration — ‘if only my lord were before the prophet in Samaria’ — is expressed as a wish (lû + jussive), reflecting her limited authority to initiate action. Her confident assertion that the prophet ‘would cure him of his leprosy’ (wᵉʾāsap ʾōtô mitstsāraʿtô) frames prophetic healing as known fact, not speculation. A slave’s testimony in an aristocratic household would normally carry little weight, but the urgency of Naaman’s condition elevates any potential solution. The transmission of prophetic reputation across ethnic and political boundaries — from an Israelite slave to an Aramean general — illustrates how YHWH’s name circulates in the international sphere.
4: Naaman’s report to the king of Aram treats the slave girl’s testimony as actionable intelligence. The chain of authority — slave girl → Naaman’s wife → Naaman → king of Aram — illustrates how information flowed through hierarchical ANE court structures. That the word of a foreign Israelite slave girl reaches the king of Aram and initiates a diplomatic mission indicates the credibility that Elisha’s reputation had achieved, even in enemy territory.
5: The gift Naaman brings for his healing is enormous by any measure: ten talents of silver (approximately 340 kg or 750 lb), six thousand shekels of gold (approximately 70 kg or 150 lb), and ten changes of clothing — a multi-year national budget for a small city-state. This level of payment reflects both the value placed on healing from tsara’at (which carried social and cultic exclusion) and the ANE convention that supernatural services — including the services of diviners, dream interpreters, and healers — commanded proportional payment. The king of Aram frames the mission as a diplomatic visit with a commercial dimension, not as a religious consultation.
6: The letter from the king of Aram to the king of Israel treats the healing transaction as a royal matter: king to king. The phrasing ‘I have sent you my servant Naaman that you may cure him’ creates a diplomatic trap: if the king of Israel cannot or will not comply, it becomes a casus belli. ANE royal correspondence (extensively documented in the Amarna letters from the 14th century BCE) frequently deployed this form — using diplomatic language to create obligations that could not easily be refused without offense.
7: The king of Israel’s tearing of garments (wayyiqraʿ ʾet-bᵉgādāyw) is the standard mourning-and-distress gesture in the Hebrew Bible, here used to express political panic rather than grief. His rhetorical question — ‘Am I God, able to kill and to make alive?’ (hăʾĕlōhîm ʾānî lᵉmāmît ûlᵉhaḥăyôt) — inadvertently makes the correct theological point: healing leprosy is a divine prerogative. His suspicion that the request is a pretext for war reflects the volatile political context of the Aramean-Israelite relationship (cf. 1 Kgs 20; 22). He does not think of Elisha, who is the actual locus of divine power.
8: Elisha’s message to the king — ‘Let him come to me, and he shall know that there is a prophet in Israel’ (wᵉyēdaʿ kî yēš nābîʾ bᵉyiśrāʾēl) — reframes the encounter from a political to a prophetic event. The phrase ‘there is a prophet in Israel’ asserts that Israel’s real resource in this situation is not royal diplomacy but the presence of a man of God. Elisha inserts himself into the diplomatic chain and takes responsibility for the outcome — a bold prophetic claim in the face of an international crisis.
9: Naaman arrives at Elisha’s house with full military retinue — horses and chariots (bᵉsûsāyw ûbᵉriḵbô) — displaying his rank and the Aramean king’s investment in the mission. The scene is staged for maximum contrast: Aramean military power at the door of an Israelite prophet’s house. Elisha does not come out to meet him — a deliberate protocol reversal. In ANE royal and elite culture, the greater power would be received; Elisha’s non-appearance signals that the expected social hierarchies are suspended.
11: Naaman’s anger reflects the violation of honor-shame expectations: he anticipated a formal healing ritual — the prophet would ‘come out to me in person, stand and call on the name of the LORD his God, and wave his hand over the spot’ (yāṣōʾ yāṣōʾ wᵉʿāmad wᵉqārāʾ bᵉšēm yhwh ʾĕlōhāyw’). This expected gesture — hand-waving over the afflicted area — is attested in ANE magical healing texts from Mesopotamia and Egypt. Naaman expected a performance appropriate to his status as a great man; the messenger’s instruction violated both the expected ritual form and the expected social deference.
12: Naaman’s mention of the Abana and Pharpar rivers (the main rivers of Damascus, modern Barada and Nahr al-Awaj) as superior to ‘all the waters of Israel’ is not simply nationalistic pride but reflects genuine hydrological superiority: the rivers of the Damascus oasis were perennially flowing, cold mountain streams, while the Jordan was a slower, lowland river, murkier in places. From a pragmatic standpoint, Naaman’s assessment is reasonable. His rejection of the Jordan is also a rejection of what the Jordan represents — the distinctive body of water associated with Israel’s crossing into the land and Elijah’s crossing in 2 Kings 2:8.
13: Naaman’s servants address him with the honorific ‘my father’ (ʾābî) — a term of respectful address that could be used by subordinates for a senior authority figure. Their logic is a qal waḥomer: if you would do something hard for the prophet, how much more something easy? The question ‘how much more, then, when he says to you, Wash and be clean?’ reverses his expectation — the very ease of the instruction should recommend it. The servants’ counsel prevails over Naaman’s pride; in the narrative structure, it is the subordinate voices (the slave girl in v3; the servants here) who carry the true prophetic message.
14: The Jordan dipping seven times — a number of completeness in Hebrew idiom — is specifically prescribed by the prophetic word; deviating from the number (stopping at five, say) would presumably not have achieved the result. Naaman’s restoration — ‘his flesh was restored like the flesh of a young child’ (wayyāšob bᵉśārô kᵉbᵉśar naʿar qāṭōn) — uses language of reversal: what was corrupted and aging is returned to a state of youth and wholeness. This is not merely medical cure but social and cultic rehabilitation: healed from tsara’at meant re-entry into community, worship, and normal social function.
15: Naaman’s confession — ‘I know that there is no God in all the earth except in Israel’ (hinnēh-nāʾ yādaʿtî kî ʾên ʾĕlōhîm bᵉḵol-hāʾāreṣ kî ʾim-bᵉyiśrāʾēl) — is a monotheistic declaration by a foreigner, comparable to Rahab’s confession (Joshua 2:11) and the queen of Sheba’s acknowledgment (1 Kings 10:9). The returned ‘gift’ (bᵉrāḵāh, blessing) offered to Elisha indicates that Naaman now understands the healing as YHWH’s act through a prophet, not a commercial transaction. The pattern — Gentile healed, Gentile confesses YHWH — is later cited by Jesus as a sign of YHWH’s universal sovereignty (Luke 4:27).
16: Elisha’s refusal of payment — sworn by the oath ‘As the LORD lives, before whom I stand’ (ḥay-yhwh ʾăšer-ʿāmadtî lᵉpānāyw) — distinguishes prophetic service from the commercial healing that characterized professional diviners and physicians in the ANE world. Prophets in Israel were sometimes paid (1 Samuel 9:7–8; cf. Balaam), but Elisha here refuses on principle: accepting payment from Naaman would suggest that the healing was a commercial service rather than a sign of YHWH’s sovereign grace operating through the prophet. The refusal also prevents any equation of YHWH’s power with marketable Aramean goods.
18: Naaman’s request for prophetic tolerance of his continued attendance in the temple of Rimmon addresses a real dilemma for a foreign monotheist in an official position. Rimmon is the storm-deity Hadad (the name ‘Rimmon’ or Rammān means ‘Thunderer’), the principal deity of Aram-Damascus, analogous to Baal in Canaan and Adad in Mesopotamia. As commander and presumably a royal official, Naaman was expected to participate in state religious ceremonies. His request — ‘may the LORD pardon your servant in this matter’ — is not cynical compromise but a sincere anticipation of moral conflict and a request for prophetic ruling on the permissibility of his situation.
19: Elisha’s response ‘Go in peace’ (lēḵ lᵉšālôm) is a standard farewell formula but carries significant weight here: it is not a rebuke, a prohibition, or a condition. The text does not amplify with any ruling on the Rimmon question; Elisha simply dismisses Naaman in peace. This ambiguity has generated extensive discussion: some interpreters see it as tacit tolerance of accommodation under constraint; others as a simple farewell without implied endorsement. The narrative leaves the question open, perhaps deliberately. Naaman departs as a confessing monotheist navigating a complex diplomatic role — a liminal figure the text does not condemn.
20: Gehazi’s interior monologue reveals the commercial logic he applies to the encounter: his master has let Naaman ‘off without accepting what he brought’ — the massive gift of silver, gold, and clothing represents an enormous windfall foregone. The phrase ‘as the LORD lives, I will run after him and accept something from him’ parodies Elisha’s oath formula in v16 — Gehazi invokes YHWH’s name in support of the very commercial transaction Elisha refused in YHWH’s name. The irony is sharp: Gehazi claims YHWH’s authority for greed.
21: Naaman sees Gehazi running after him and steps down from his chariot to meet him — the gesture of social consideration toward someone of lower status signals Naaman’s genuine transformation. His question ‘Is everything all right?’ (hăšālôm) echoes the welfare formula of v26 and establishes the ironic contrast: Naaman, newly cleansed and confessing, extends considerate inquiry; Gehazi, standing in the prophetic household, is about to commit fraud.
22: Gehazi’s fabricated story — two young prophets have arrived and Elisha needs silver and clothing for them — invents a plausible prophetic-community need to justify the request. The ‘company of the prophets from the hill country of Ephraim’ is a geographically plausible reference; prophetic guilds were known in that region. Gehazi requests a fraction of what Naaman offered (one talent of silver vs. ten), making the deception seem modest and therefore credible. The request for clothing is particularly apt, since Naaman’s gift had specifically included it.
23: Naaman’s generosity in insisting on giving two talents rather than the one requested reflects his gratitude and the enormous surplus of what he brought. Two talents of silver is still a staggering sum — equivalent to perhaps 70 kg of silver — far more than needed for any plausible prophetic community need. Naaman ties the silver in two bags and assigns two of his own servants to carry them back with Gehazi — a courtesy to the assumed prophets of Israel, and an inadvertent witness to Gehazi’s deception.
24: ‘The hill’ (hāʿōpel) refers to a specific elevated part of the approach to the city — possibly the Ophel, though the northern Israel context makes a different location more likely. Gehazi takes the bags from Naaman’s servants, deposits them inside, and sends the servants back — all before presenting himself to Elisha. The concealment and the sequential actions indicate premeditation, not impulsive theft. Gehazi is methodically managing the cover of his deception.
25: Elisha’s question ‘Where have you been, Gehazi?’ and Gehazi’s denial ‘Your servant hasn’t been anywhere’ sets up the prophetic exposure that follows. The denial uses a strong Hebrew idiom of absence: lōʾ-hālaḵ ʿabdᵉḵā ʾānnâ wᵉʾānnâ, ‘your servant did not go anywhere at all.’ The redundancy intensifies the lie. Elisha’s knowledge of Gehazi’s movements — ‘did not my heart go with you?’ (v26) — reflects the prophetic omniscience that makes deception futile; the prophet sees what the servant thinks is hidden.
26: ‘Is this the time to accept silver, clothing, olive groves, vineyards, sheep, oxen, and servants?’ (hāʿēt lāqaḥat et-hakkesep wᵉlāqaḥat bᵉgādîm) — Elisha’s list expands far beyond what Gehazi actually received, describing the full range of wealth accumulation. The prophetic critique targets not just this specific act but the mercenary orientation it represents. The ‘time’ (ʿēt) argument draws on prophetic calendrical consciousness: this was the moment when Naaman had been healed and confessed YHWH; commodifying that moment by accepting payment corrupts the witness value of the healing.
27: The transfer of Naaman’s leprosy to Gehazi and his descendants is the judicial sentence that closes the account. ‘Cling to you and your descendants forever’ (tidbaq-bᵉḵā ûbᵉzarʿᵃḵā lᵉʿôlām) — the same condition Naaman was healed of becomes the permanent mark of the servant’s betrayal. The irony is structural: Gehazi traded the healing miracle for silver, and the miracle is reversed against him. Naaman goes away white-skinned no more (wᵉhûʾ mᵉṣōrāʿ kaššāleg); Gehazi goes away ‘white as snow’ — carrying what the Aramean commander left behind.
